steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content:
Before the blank: ...veins that provide structural support — a complete independent clause.
After the blank: Decreasing in thickness from the center to the edges, these veins allow... — another complete independent clause with an introductory participial phrase.

Two independent clauses must be separated by terminal punctuation (or a semicolon).

steps.title: Apply the rule
steps.content:
Two independent clauses cannot be joined by a comma alone; they need a period, semicolon, or comma + FANBOYS conjunction.

A — "support, decreasing": A comma alone joins two independent clauses — a comma splice. "Decreasing in thickness..." is a participial phrase starting a full new independent clause ("these veins allow..."). Fails.

B — "support decreasing": No punctuation at all between two independent clauses — a fused sentence. Fails.

C — "support. Decreasing": A period ends the first independent clause. "Decreasing in thickness from the center to the edges" is then an introductory participial phrase for the new sentence "these veins allow the leaves to maintain their large size." Correct.

D — "support, while decreasing": Adding "while" makes the second clause subordinate, but the result — "while decreasing in thickness from the center to the edges, these veins allow..." — still follows a comma splice off the first independent clause "...provide structural support." Fails.
